title: incident-bot: lightweight diagnostic toolbelt + ephemeral debug sessions for responders
preview: Motivation: on-call responders repeatedly run the same set of manual diagnostics when an alert fires (span tailing, recent-error log windows, lightweight heap/alloc snapshots, and quick-curl checks). This PR introduces a compact 'diagnostic toolbelt' exposed by incident-bot and an ephemeral debug session mode that lets responders run short-lived, auditable diagnostics without escalation to infra. Goals: reduce MTTD by providing one-click diagnostics, keep operations safe via TTLs and RBAC checks, and ensure cost caps through soft quotas and telemetry. What this change does: 1) Adds a new /v1/incidents/{id}/toolbelt API surface that aggregates a configurable set of diagnostics (logs tail, span tail, recent-errors, quick-rerun of failing healthcheck endpoints, and a heap-lite snapshot request). 2) Implements ephemeral debug sessions: a generated session token with a 15-minute TTL that grants scoped read-only access to targeted logs/traces and a sandboxed runner to perform pre-approved diagnostic commands. Sessions are recorded to the audit log and emit a session lifecycle event for billing/telemetry. 3) New UI affordance: a Toolbelt button in the incident timeline that opens the toolbelt panel (web/src/components/toolbelt/*) and a compact modal for ephemeral session creation with an estimated cost meter. 4) CLI helpers: incident-botctl toolbelt start --incident <id> --profile quick-check that opens a short-lived session and prints the session token and telemetry link. 5) Safety and Governance: RBAC checks against SSO groups, KMS-wrapped session secrets, per-tenant soft quotas, request rate-limiting, and a conservative whitelist of allowed runner commands. 6) Observability: new metrics (toolbelt.sessions.created, toolbelt.diagnostics.count, toolbelt.session.duration) and tracing spans to measure tool impact on latency/free resources. Implementation notes: backend changes in cmd/api/handler_toolbelt.go and internal/toolbelt/* which provide the orchestration, session manager, and audit plumbing; UI changes in web/src/components/toolbelt and web/src/styles; lightweight runner implemented as a new containerless job executor (internal/runner/lightweight) that runs commands in a hardened sandbox (no outbound network by default except to whitelisted endpoints). Diffs intentionally keep payloads small; large log downloads are paginated and pre-signed links have a short expiry. Testing: unit tests added for session lifecycle and RBAC checks (internal/toolbelt/session_test.go), e2e harness extends incident-sim to validate TTL expiry and audit log entries (test/e2e/toolbelt_flow.yaml). Migration and rollout: feature gated behind a runtime flag INCIDENT_TOOLBELT_ENABLED false by default; small canary rollout to 10% of orgs for first week with debug logging; telemetry hooks added to detect anomalous usage patterns and auto-disable soft-quotas if hit. Rollback plan: flip INCIDENT_TOOLBELT_ENABLED off, invalidate active sessions via session manager kill-all, and remove UI button via a targeted client config flag. References: ENG-4127 (design doc), ENG-4198 (safety review), and an internal runbook template updated to include toolbelt guidance.

title: heuristic DAG runbook compiler and local repro hints
preview: Motivation: Oncall responders frequently need a concise, executable set of steps that adapt to observed telemetry. This PR introduces a heuristic DAG compiler that converts high-level runbook templates and telemetry predicates into compact conditional playbooks plus short local repro hints (minimal traces, query snippets, and service call stubs). Design: 1) A lightweight DAG IR captures nodes (check, query, mitigation, escalate) and edges with guard predicates; 2) Compiler heuristics prune nodes unlikely to apply given recent telemetry (configurable thresholds) and collapse linear chains into single composite actions to reduce cognitive load; 3) Local repro hint extractor synthesizes a minimal set of queries/trace spans and a docker-compose sandbox recipe (when available) to reproduce the symptom locally; 4) Safe-mode runner executes a dry-run passing through the DAG with simulated telemetry to validate branch coverage. Checklist: - added DAG IR and in-memory evaluator; - implemented three pruning heuristics (frequency, recency, impact score); - added repro-hint extractor with sanitizers and redaction rules; - added unit tests for compiler, evaluator, and sanitizer; - added integration test that runs compiler on 20 existing runbooks and compares size reduction and coverage; - added small CLI `slo runbook-compile --input ... --dry-run` and a `slo runbook-local-repro` helper; - documented new config knobs in docs/runbooks/README.md; - feature gated behind feature flag `runbook_dag_compiler` and rollout plan in this PR. Testing & rollout: Unit tests pass locally and in CI. Integration runs showed a median runbook size reduction of 42% for the sampled corpus while preserving coverage for configured test prompts. Canary rollout: enabled for a 5% oncall group for two weeks, monitor false-positive rate and responder feedback. Migration: existing runbooks are still usable; CLI and API will accept both legacy and DAG format for 3 months before deprecating legacy authoring path. Safety and security: repro hints are sanitized using the project's redaction policy; any hint that would include PII or secrets is replaced with placeholders and an automated review flag is raised. Impact: intended to speed triage and lower MTTR by delivering more focused instructions and an ability to run quick local replays.

title: runbook-telemetry-heuristics-and-blindspot-prober
preview:
Problem: our runbook suggestion flow surface-level checks (restarts, scaling, config flags) but frequently misses gaps in telemetry — 'blindspots' where a missing or mis-tagged metric/tracing span causes responders to chase symptoms. This PR adds a lightweight heuristics engine that analyzes incoming incident context + historical telemetry expectations and surfaces likely blindspots, and a safe 'blindspot prober' that can run read-only checks against telemetry pipelines to validate assumptions.

What this does (high level):
- Adds a telemetry heuristics module to incident-bot that computes a blindspot_score for an incident based on absent expected signals (metrics/traces/log markers) and signal-quality indicators (sample rates, tag variance, TTL gaps).
- Introduces a blindspot prober that runs ephemeral, read-only queries (metrics histogram checks, span-presence checks, and tag-resolution sanity probes) against configured telemetry backends (Prometheus/Graphite, Jaeger/Tempo, and S3/Log-aggregation). The prober is gated and runs with sampling & rate limits.
- Enhances runbook suggestion generation: when blindspot_score > threshold, incident-bot injects concrete probe steps into the suggested runbook (queries to run, tags to check, quick remediation stubs).
- Adds feature flagging (incident_bot.features.blindspot_detection) and a per-org sampling config (orgConfig.blindspotSamplingPct) to control rollout.
- Telemetry: emits new metrics incident_bot.blindspot_score and incident_bot.blindspot_probes_run with labels (org, backend, result).

Design notes and safety:
- Prober is read-only and only executes queries that are explicitly marked safe in the backend config. We enforce a strict query whitelist per-backend; arbitrary queries are forbidden.
- Sampling default is 2% across all free-tier orgs and 10% for verified customers; can be tuned via org config.
- To avoid noisy oncall behavior we produce a separate 'blindspot advisory' card and do NOT auto-trigger remediation.

Implementation summary (commits):
- commit 1: add telemetry heuristics engine and blindspot scoring prototype (internal API + tests)
- commit 2: implement blindspot prober, backend adapters for Prometheus and Jaeger, config knobs, and telemetry metrics
- commit 3: runbook suggestion integration, CLI flags, docs, and unit/integration tests

Changed files (high level):
- pkg/heuristics/blindspot.go — new scoring engine (core algorithm with configurable signal expectations and TTL checks).
- pkg/prober/prober.go — orchestrates safe probes, sampling, and result normalization.
- pkg/backends/prometheus_adapter.go — safe query wrapper and whitelist enforcement.
- pkg/backends/jaeger_adapter.go — cheap span-presence checks and tag-resolve helper.
- cmd/incident-bot/flags.go — new feature flags and runtime knobs.
- webui/runbooks/suggestions_renderer.go — inject advisory cards and probe steps.
- docs/operational/runbooks.md — documentation and rollout checklist.

Small illustrative snippet added to blindspot.go to document expected signal config (kept short in PR):

// expectedSignals: map[service] = ["http_request_total", "db_query_latency_ms"]
// TTL indicates how long we expect a signal to be present before flagging a gap

Review conversation (summarized; chronological):
- Alexei Petrov (reviewer): "Nice approach. Two concerns: 1) sampling defaults — 2% seems low for signal coverage on small teams; can we make it org-configurable and increase for verified customers? 2) whitelist enforcement: how do we prevent ops from adding expensive queries?"
- Maya Chen (author): "Answered inline: sampling is org-configurable (see orgConfig.blindspotSamplingPct) and defaults are conservative; updated docs and added verified-customer override. For whitelist, adapters require an admin-level 'safe_query' entry and queries are introspected for their selector type (histogram/counter only); arbitrary PromQL with subqueries is disallowed. Added tests for blacklist attempts.")
- Priya Raman (reviewer): "Security review request: ensure credentials used by the prober are scoped read-only and rotated. Also request an opt-out for orgs that do not permit telemetry queries."
- Maya Chen: "Added README section on credential scoping and a per-org disable switch (orgConfig.disableBlindspotProbes). Also added RBAC check in prober bootstrap.")
- Jordan Blake (approver): "Looks good; tests are solid. Two small nits on naming and a log-level change. Approved after nits fixed."

CI checks (timeline):
- unit-tests — pass
- integration-tests (telemetry adapters mocked) — pass
- lint — pass (fixed a couple of style issues in prober.go)
- e2e — flaky initially (timeout in Jaeger adapter test), reran and passed

Merge outcome: merged on 2026-03-02 via squash merge. Release notes appended. Feature flag disabled by default; rollout plan in docs/operational/runbooks.md.

Release notes (for changelog):
- incident-bot: add blindspot detection and safe telemetry prober to surface missing telemetry signals and augment runbooks with concrete probe steps. Feature gated and off by default.

Testing and QA notes:
- Added unit tests for scoring thresholds, prober sampling, and backend whitelist enforcement.
- Added integration tests that spin up lightweight mock Prometheus/Jaeger endpoints; e2e smoke test included in CI.
- QA run requested by Priya; sign-off included in review thread.

title: inline-lint-orb-and-dependency-choreography: compact presubmit adapter + churn detector
preview:
Motivation: our presubmit and lint surface has grown many small, slightly divergent composite actions and ad-hoc dependency rollup jobs. This PR introduces an "inline lint orb" (composite action pattern kept in-repo for now) and a small "dependency choreography" toolset that groups dependency updates into ecosystem-aware batches, plus a churn detector to reduce noisy dep churn PRs. Goals: 1) shrink presubmit job graph and reduce redundant installs, 2) create a single reusable lint adapter that supports language-specific shims, 3) reduce dependency churn and make rollups deterministic, 4) provide a light-weight cache priming step to improve presubmit latency.

What changed (high level):
- Added a composite-ish action under .github/actions/inline-lint-orb that centralizes linters (eslint, go vet, rustfmt) with a small shim to select language adapters at runtime. This replaces three separate lint composite actions used across presubmits.
- Introduced tools/dep_choreographer (Go) which inspects lockfiles across packages, groups updates by ecosystem (npm, pip, go) and emits a deterministic rollup manifest. The rollup manifest is used by a new workflow job to produce a single rollup PR instead of many per-package updates.
- Added tools/churn_detector (Python) that runs in CI to compute a churn score for dependency PRs (based on number of transitive updates + touch frequency). If churn > threshold the job annotates the PR and recommends a staged rollout instead of automated merge.
- Modified .github/workflows/presubmit.yml to use the inline lint orb and split the presubmit into three fast lanes: quick-lint (fast), unit-smoke (short unit tests), and full-presubmit (longer integration tests). A cache-warm step runs in parallel to reduce cold-install latency.
- Small release automation tweak: release/prepare step now skips dependency rollups from the choreographer unless the linear ticket is referenced (prevents accidental release noise).
- Docs: docs/ci/README.md documents the new presubmit lanes, lint orb usage, and choreographer config.

Description/details (continuous): The inline lint orb is a single entrypoint action that accepts a "languages" list and a "shim_version" variable. Internally it uses language-specific adapters stored in .github/actions/inline-lint-orb/adapters/ which are small shell wrappers that invoke the project's canonical linter commands. The presubmit uses a language detection heuristic to avoid running unnecessary linters on PRs that touch only unrelated subtrees. The dep_choreographer performs analysis across package lockfiles (package-lock.json, poetry.lock, go.sum) and emits a rollup manifest manifest/dep-rollup-<sha>.yaml which the workflow consumes to create a single rollup branch. The churn_detector computes churn = transient_update_count * log(age_in_days + 1) / touched_packages and annotates PRs with the score and recommended action. This is intentionally conservative—high churn does not block merges, it annotates and triggers the staged merge flow if auto-merge is enabled.

Testing & CI: Presubmit pipeline initially failed on flaky integration tests (known flake: tests/network/test_routing.py) which caused a rerun; after isolating the flake the presubmit succeeded. New unit tests added for churn_detector (tools/churn_detector/tests/test_score.py) and integration smoke for dep_choreographer (tools/dep_choreographer/integration/verify_rollup.sh). Local developer flow: developers can run ./tools/dep_choreographer/cli rollup --dry-run to preview rollup manifests. There is a small linting migration script that updates repo-level linters to the orb-compatible entrypoints.

Rollout plan: merge to main behind feature flag (CI uses env REDWOOD_CI_LINT_ORB=true), monitor presubmit latency and number of lint jobs via the Redwood Console dashboard for 72 hours. After stability window, flip feature flag and deprecate the legacy per-language composite actions.

Related: Linear ENG-2741 (converge presubmit adapters), ENG-2789 (dependency rollup strategy). See internal doc: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-2741 and design doc: https://redwood-internal.company/docs/ci/choreographer-design (internal).

title: Surge orchestration conductor and tenant telemetry bridge
preview: Motivation: transient multi-tenant microbursts frequently cause noisy autoscaler churn and downstream p95 latency spikes. This PR introduces a lightweight Surge Orchestration Conductor that coordinates short-lived surge commitments across pools, plus a tenant telemetry bridge that exports compact utilization fingerprints for correlated analysis. The goal is to reduce reactive overprovisioning, improve tenant isolation during bursts, and provide richer signals for the autoscaler without increasing metric cardinality. Changes include: 1) a conductor control loop that accepts tentative surge offers, serializes commit windows, and emits lease intents to pool allocators; 2) a tenant isolation gate that computes a per-tenant isolation score and enforces soft caps when isolation threshold breached; 3) a utilization-fingerprint exporter that samples the per-tenant seqlen/throughput/burstiness vector every 10s and emits a compact hash+sketch to telemetry to enable correlating degradations with surge events; 4) back-pressure integration with the request router so surge commits can be denied fast when global headroom is low; 5) config knobs behind runtime feature flags for rollout and tuning. This PR includes unit tests for conductor decision logic, integration tests for coordinator+pool handshake using the test harness, and a small e2e canary that runs against a dedicated staging fleet. See linked Linear ENG-4281 for heuristics tuning and SRE runbook updates. Rollout plan: feature-flagged canary on 10% of production tenants for 72h, escalate if p95 latency falls or tenant error rates climb. Backout plan: flip feature flags off to revert conductor and gate; telemetry bridge is read-only and safe to keep on. Impact: expected to reduce autoscaler-triggered warm starts by ~12-25% in observed synthetic surges and reduce p95 by ~30-70ms in staging traces. TODO: follow-up to expose per-tenant isolation diagnostics in Console (ENG-4309).

Changes include: 1) a conductor control loop that accepts tentative surge offers, serializes commit windows, and emits lease intents to pool allocators; 2) a tenant isolation gate that computes a per-tenant isolation score and enforces soft caps when isolation threshold breached; 3) a utilization-fingerprint exporter that samples the per-tenant seqlen/throughput/burstiness vector every 10s and emits a compact hash+sketch to telemetry to enable correlating degradations with surge events; 4) back-pressure integration with the request router so surge commits can be denied fast when global headroom is low; 5) config knobs behind runtime feature flags for rollout and tuning. This PR includes unit tests for conductor decision logic, integration tests for coordinator+pool handshake using the test harness, and a small e2e canary that runs against a dedicated staging fleet. See linked Linear ENG-4281 for heuristics tuning and SRE runbook updates. Rollout plan: feature-flagged canary on 10% of production tenants for 72h, escalate if p95 latency falls or tenant error rates climb. Backout plan: flip feature flags off to revert conductor and gate; telemetry bridge is read-only and safe to keep on. Impact: expected to reduce autoscaler-triggered warm starts by ~12-25% in observed synthetic surges and reduce p95 by ~30-70ms in staging traces. TODO: follow-up to expose per-tenant isolation diagnostics in Console (ENG-4309).

title: tooling/execution: concurrency safeguards and tool-result-injection ergonomics
preview: Motivation: Customers integrating complex tool chains reported flaky outputs when multiple tools ran in parallel and tool results were injected back into the model stream. This PR standardizes execution semantics for multi-tool calls, adds runtime safeguards for parallelism, and harmonizes behavior with our OpenAI-compat surface. Summary of changes: 1) Executor: introduce a per-request "tool-parallelism" cap (configurable at API/route level) that bounds concurrent tool invocations and provides backpressure hooks to the scheduler; 2) Injection protocol: formalize a deterministic injection ordering when multiple tool results arrive within the same model turn and attach an idempotency token to each injected result so retries do not duplicate content; 3) Retry semantics: new retry policy enum (none, best-effort, strict-idempotent) exposed via the API and respected by the runtime and OpenAI-compat shim; 4) Streaming stability: when streaming responses include tool-calls, we now atomically flush tool-invocation markers to the stream and gracefully resume generation once injected results are committed to the KV cache; 5) OpenAI-compat updates: function-calling payloads now accept an optional "execution_hints" map with parallelism and idempotency hints that the compat layer translates into our internal routing and executor options; 6) Observability: emit new metrics (tool.invocations.active, tool.invocations.queued, tool.injection.latency) and traces annotated with execution_hints for easier debugging. Implementation notes: - Executor changes are confined to runtime/executor/* and api/handlers/tool_call_handler.go; the executor preserves the previous scheduling algorithm but applies per-request parallelism caps before dispatching tool workers. - Injection order uses a deterministic comparator: (request_tool_sequence, tool_priority, start_timestamp). This avoids nondeterministic interleaving in model-visible outputs. - Idempotency tokens are short-lived UUIDs persisted in the request context and kept in the per-request token-cache; replayed tool results with matching token are ignored by the injection layer. - The OpenAI compatibility shim accepts additional fields but falls back to previous behavior when execution_hints are omitted to preserve backward compatibility. Checklist: - [x] Design doc updated with sequence diagrams and failure modes (ENG-1523) - [x] Unit tests for ordering and idempotency - [x] Integration tests simulating multi-tool fan-out and parallel retries - [x] Metrics/tracing added and tested in canary - [x] Backwards compatibility verified for existing function-calling clients - [x] Docs snippet for API surface added Notes on tradeoffs: We intentionally chose a conservative default parallelism cap of 4 to reduce burst load on downstream tool services; customers can raise this per-route with a warning in the console. The idempotency token approach requires minimal state but does not protect against cross-request duplicate side-effects (we call this out in the design doc). Migration: No action required from existing users; new execution_hints fields are additive. For customers wanting deterministic injection ordering across retries, they should include explicit tool priority in their tool manifest. Rollout plan: Canary on a small percentage of hosted traffic for one week with annotated traces and enhanced logging; force-enable flag included to accelerate rollouts for enterprise tenants. Backwards compatibility: tests included to ensure older SDKs (OpenAI-compat) do not break. Risk and mitigations: The biggest risk is increased latency when a tool result arrives slightly after generation — mitigated by configurable timeouts and an async-fallback policy that allows the generator to continue with a best-effort placeholder if strict-idempotent is not set. Local testing: see integration test harness in tests/integration/tool_invoke_fanout_test.go. CI: all unit/integration checks passed after two reruns (flaky test fix in metrics). Merge outcome: squash-merged to main with commit 0a9f3d2 (executor: add parallelism cap + injection protocol).

title: stabilize-cuda-graph-capture-with-deterministic-kernel-mapping-and-shape-buckets
preview:
Motivation: Recent rolling workloads exposed flakiness and performance regressions in CUDA graph capture/replay under our continuous-batching scheduler. Two root causes were observed: (1) non-deterministic kernel selection when capture order varied across worker threads, causing spurious re-captures and kernel mismatches; (2) overly fine-grained shape boundaries that forced frequent warmups for near-identical shapes.

Summary: This PR introduces a deterministic kernel mapping table persisted per-worker for the life of the process (with safe invalidation), and a shape-bucketing fallback that groups close sequence/kv-cache shapes into conservative buckets used during capture selection. The scheduler is updated to prefer forming capture groups with identical buckets where possible, and we add a lightweight capture-time verification check that triggers a localized replay fallback instead of a full capture redo when mismatch is transient.

What changed (high level):
- runtime/cuda_graphs/mapper.cc/.h: new KernelMapper that records capture-time kernel bindings and exposes a stable lookup keyed by (arch, model-id, op-profile, bucket-id). Mapper persists mappings in-process and exposes safe eviction hooks.
- runtime/scheduler/capture_grouper.*: scheduler hinting to prefer bucket-consistent grouping; heuristic respects latency SLOs and will not block dispatch for grouping beyond a short window.
- runtime/cuda_graphs/buckets.h: conservative bucketing implementation (quantized seq-len, kv-cache hit/miss, batch-size) + utilities for bucket stability metrics.
- runtime/cuda_graphs/replay_guard.cc: introduced lightweight verification that checks kernel signatures at replay time and falls back to replay-only path for affected threads (avoids global re-capture).
- tests/integ/cuda_graphs/*: new deterministic capture integration tests and a stressbench that simulates shape-churn workloads.

Checklist: [x] Unit tests added for KernelMapper behavior; [x] Integration test simulating interleaved captures; [x] Benchmarks run on perf-lab (see results below); [x] Docs note for internal runtime ops/troubleshooting.

Benchmarks (perf-lab, NVIDIA A100, mixed prompt distribution with tail-heavy 2-8 token growth):
- 95th p99 tail latency (first 60s of warmup): median improvement 12% across 3 runs.
- Number of full re-captures per minute: dropped from 14 -> 1 under the simulated shape-churn workload.
- Steady-state token throughput unchanged; warmup-time variance reduced by ~40%.

Testing notes: Added deterministic seeds for capture order in tests to catch previous non-determinism. Stressbench reproduces prior regression (interleaved batches with slight seq-len variations) and now validates no more than one full re-capture per minute in the scenario.

Rollout plan: Feature gated behind runtime flag cuda_graph.stable_mapping (default on for dedicated and hosted fleets in this rollout). Gradual rollout: perf-canary -> half of Dedicated clusters -> all. We will monitor capture_mismatch_rate and full_recapture_count dashboards and revert if sign of regression > 1%.

Risk & mitigations: Introducing an in-process mapper state carries memory and staleness risk. We keep mappings lightweight (small key) and add TTL and explicit invalidation hooks on model reloads or quantization profile changes. The replay_guard ensures a safe localized fallback if a mapping proves stale.

Related: ENG-1423 is the parent for tracking capture regressions; INT-4332 captures the customer incident that motivated the fix.

title: Introduce quiet-window sampling and Mosaic metric adapters for token burst noise suppression
preview: Motivation: production token bursts from background jobs and chatfanout generate high-cardinality short-lived noise that skews percentile-based alerts and dilutes useful tail traces. This PR introduces two complementary pieces: 1) a configurable quiet-window sampler that temporarily de-emphasizes bursts matching short high-throughput token patterns so SLO signals reflect real user-facing latency/cost instead of backend batch noise; 2) Mosaic metric adapters, a lightweight adapter layer that coalesces related token-phase counters, kernel latency samples, and queueing gauges into a small set of composite metrics and tags for dashboards and alert rules. Approach: quiet-window sampling detects burst windows per-route and per-tenant using a sliding token-rate estimator and applies an adjustable attenuation factor to sampling probability and cost attribution for spans that are classified as background-burst. Mosaic adapters implement a mapping layer in the telemetry pipeline that emits aggregated metrics (mosaic.token_rate, mosaic.kernel_p50, mosaic.queueing_p95) alongside original granular metrics to preserve observability. Safety and rollout: default behavior is off; feature flags gate behavior at tenant-level with conservative defaults; we ship an opt-in rollout for internal tenants for 2 weeks, collect comparison diffs, then flip progressively. Backwards compatibility: no change to existing metric names or span schemas; adapters emit new mosaic.* series only. Monitoring and testing: added comparison dashboards, a synthetic burst generator for canary verification, and unit+integration tests. Impact: expected reduction in false-positive SLO alerts for noisy tenants and clearer long-tail attributions for genuine slow invocations. See notes and examples below for configuration and thresholds.

title: tenant-graceful-shepherd-and-circuit-unplug-hotfix
preview: Motivation: production incidents in the past 48 hours revealed two correlated failure modes during tenant unplug (customer-initiated detach and scheduled maintenance): 1) abrupt KV cache eviction left in-flight requests failing with 503s; 2) regional circuit state machines could flip to OPEN due to transient health probe flaps and then remain sticky, causing cascading regional failover and load oscillation. This PR provides a minimal, low-risk hotfix that introduces a ’graceful shepherd’ flow for tenant unplug and hardens the circuit state transition logic to tolerate transient probe failures. Summary of changes: - Introduces a tenant-unplug coordinator that drains inflight shared KV cache sessions and transitions tenant routing into a ’soft-standby’ window (configurable ttl) before full detach. This avoids dropping requests and allows in-progress batches to complete. - Adds a transient-probe debounce layer and bounded reopen backoff to the regional circuit breaker so a sequence of short probe failures does not permanently open circuits. - Adds explicit telemetry and audit events for unplug start/finish and for circuit soft-deny transitions to aid post-incident analysis. Rollout strategy: - Deploy as a hotfix to main, then staggered rollout to release branches: v2026.02-release and v2025.12-legacy. - Feature gate for graceful-shepherd enabled by default for dedicated and private customers; can be toggled per-customer via tenant flags for additional vetting. Testing and validation: - Unit tests for coordinator state transitions (drain -> soft-standby -> detached) and for circuit debounce paths. - Integration test harness simulating concurrent request streams during unplug; validated no 5xx spike in local perf lab. - Canary deployment to a subset of regions (us-west-2 then eu-west-1) with streaming telemetry checks and automatic rollback on error-rate delta > +1.5% over baseline. Backport and urgency: This is a hotfix tied to customer-impacting incidents (SUP-4312). Targeted for immediate merge and backport. Risk: Low-medium. Changes are conservative, mostly additive control logic and feature-guarded. KV drain uses existing eviction APIs and respects current SLO prioritization. Notes: This PR is intentionally small and focused to minimize blast radius; follow-up PRs will (a) add a formal visibility page in Console and (b) provide an admin tool to trigger manual shepherd windows. References: Linear ENG-1952, incident notes SUP-4312.

title: Introduce frugal regimen defaults catalog and dry-run econ simulator
preview: Context and motivation: We need a first-class, opinionated set of 'frugal regimen' defaults that are safe for managed tenants to adopt out-of-the-box and a simulator that can estimate unit-econ impact before rollout. This PR introduces: 1) a defaults catalog (catalog/v1) that encodes batching, prefill/cache hysteresis, and quantization profile suggestions as named presets; 2) a CLI dry-run simulator (cmd/econ-dryrun) that replays representative tenant request traces against a chosen preset to produce P50/P95 latency, token-cost, and estimated per-request unit econ; 3) telemetry hooks and tracing tags so the console can visualize simulated vs actual outcomes; 4) migration and schema validation tooling to safely add presets in future. Design notes: - Presets are deliberately conservative: safe fall-through to baseline model and non-destructive cache settings. - Simulator runs in a sandbox mode (does not change production routing or caches) and supports multiple replay modes (sampled, continuous, synthetic). - Quantization profiles are annotations (not enforced) that map workload shape heuristics to candidate qconfigs; actual enforcement is left to separate rollout ops. Checklist: - [x] Defaults catalog schema and validator - pkg/econ/catalog.go, internal/config/catalog_schema.yaml - [x] CLI dry-run simulator and replay engine - cmd/econ-dryrun/main.go, internal/sim/replay.go - [x] Telemetry events and console knobs - observability/metrics/econ_sim_metrics.go - [x] Unit tests and integration smoke - tests/econ_sim/* - [x] Docs: console guide and how-to (docs/ops/frugal-regimen.md) Example usage: econ-dryrun --preset frugal-small --trace-id tenant-123 --mode sampled --lookback 7d -> writes a simulation.json with latency/p99, token-cost, and a suggested rollout risk score Migration: new presets added to configs/catalog/frugal-presets.yaml. The catalog loader validates schema on startup and will emit a non-fatal warning if unknown fields are present. Rollout plan: - Stage 1 (canary): enable simulator for SRE/product to validate top-10 tenants (manual) - Stage 2 (opt-in): expose preset list in Console under "Cost Optimization" with an opt-in dry-run button - Stage 3 (suggestions): surface top-2 recommended presets in the Cost Advisor UI after 2 successful dry-runs Backwards compatibility: presets are additive and the loader respects older schemas. No runtime behavior change unless operators explicitly promote a preset to tenant config. Security notes: simulator runs with read-only access to trace/archive storage and does not provision GPU; quant annotations do not change model binaries. Performance: simulator is CPU-bound and can run in the control plane; we've added rate-limits and queuing for multi-tenant runs. Known limitations and follow-ups: - Integration with the rollout engine (A/B canary) will be a follow-up (ENG-8830) - Add cost sensitivity estimation per token-type (short vs long) - Provide a webhook-based callback for downstream CI to gate config promotion Commit summary: 6 commits - Add catalog schema and loader (initial) - Implement frugal-presets YAML and default entries - Add econ-dryrun CLI and replay engine - Integrate telemetry and metrics for simulation runs - Tests + docs - Fix config edge case: allow legacy sample_rate key Files changed (high level): - pkg/econ/catalog.go: new loader, validator - configs/catalog/frugal-presets.yaml: initial presets (small, medium, burst-safe) - cmd/econ-dryrun/*: CLI entrypoint, replay engine, output formatter - internal/sim/replay.go: core replay and cost estimator - observability/metrics/econ_sim_metrics.go: simulation duration, error counts, sample histograms - docs/ops/frugal-regimen.md: operator guide - tests/econ_sim/*: unit and integration cases Testing notes: - Local unit tests: go test ./pkg/econ - Simulated replay runs produced expected p95 delta <= 5% vs baseline simulator harness - E2E on staging: ran dry-run on two tenants and compared predicted vs measured (within expected tolerance) Release notes: Adds a new defaults catalog for cost-conscious tenants and a CLI simulator to estimate economic impact. No automatic changes to tenant configs; opt-in only. Risk and mitigations: the preset validator is conservative and will not accept any preset that disables cache entirely; CLI defaults to a read-only sandbox. This PR intentionally focuses on introducing safe primitives and UI-facing telemetry hooks rather than automatic enforcement or rollout. For reviewers: pay attention to catalog schema changes and the simulator's sampling logic (internal/sim/replay.go).

title: Orchestra pausepoint harness with transparent rewind and cohort quarantine
preview:
Motivation:
We need a deterministic way to hold, inspect, and safely rewind model rollout traffic for live canaries and A/B experiments without losing session-affinity or producing silent regressions. Current pause mechanics are ad-hoc (manual scaling, pod cordon) and opaque in telemetry which makes post-hoc root cause and safe rewind labor-intensive.

What this PR does (summary):
- Introduces a Pausepoint state machine inside the orchestrator that exposes explicit holdpoints for staged rollouts. Holdpoints can be triggered by policy (telemetry gates) or manually from the Console.
- Implements a Cohort Quarantine controller that isolates affected user slices when a holdpoint is entered; quarantined cohorts are routed to a shadow or golden variant, and traffic is stamped for replay.
- Adds Transparent Rewind: deterministic session-preserving replay of quarantined traffic against previous model snapshots with side-by-side scoring so operators can compare deltas before committing an automatic revert.
- Expands telemetry: every pausepoint decision now emits a decision_reason tag, cohort_id, and a deterministic trace id that ties replay sessions to original requests.
- Includes operator runbook and console UI for inspecting pausepoint state, kicking off a rewind, or escalating to manual rollback.

Design notes:
- Pausepoint lifecycle: OPEN -> HOLDING -> QUARANTINED -> REPLAYING -> RESOLVED (COMMIT/REVERT). Transitions are guarded by timeouts and safety tokens so long-running holds can be auto-escalated.
- Quarantine semantics: quarantined cohorts are isolated at routing layer; their requests are mirrored to both candidate and golden models. Mirrors are rate-limited and tagged for replay capture.
- Rewind integrity: replay is performed preserving session cookies and KV cache pointers when available; where exact replay is impossible (external side-effects) we mark the trace as non-deterministic and exclude it from automatic revert decisions.

Checklist:
- [x] Pausepoint state machine + controller implementation
- [x] Cohort quarantine routing + mirror capture
- [x] Deterministic replay harness for transparent rewind
- [x] Telemetry tags and Console UI for pausepoint inspection
- [x] Integration tests covering holdpoint flow and replay verification
- [x] Runbook and operator playbook for incident handling

Backward compatibility:
- Pausepoints are opt-in via a new CRD (Pausepoint) and a feature flag. Default behavior for existing rollouts is unchanged.

Risks and mitigations:
- Increased memory footprint for mirrored requests: mitigated with streaming mirrors and configurable retention TTLs.
- Replay non-determinism could produce spurious signals: we surface non-deterministic flags and exclude affected traces from automated scoring.

How to test locally:
1) bootstrap local orchestrator with feature flag enabled (scripts/local_replay_fixture.sh)
2) create a Pausepoint CR using examples/rollouts/cohort_quarantine.yml
3) trigger a canary and send test traffic; use Console PausepointPanel to enter HOLD state
4) initiate Replay and watch telemetry spans emitted to local Jaeger

Related work and tickets: ENG-3245 (pausepoint design), ENG-3310 (transparent rewind prototype), SUP-4421 (customer incident: noisy canary requiring manual quarantine)

Notes on scope: This change focuses on rollout control-plane and routing glue; it does not attempt to modify model runtime kernels or KV cache serialization across versions — those are future follow-ups (linked in design doc).

title: hook-priority-scheduler-and-template-dependency-resolution
preview: Introduce a hook priority scheduler and deterministic template dependency resolution for the private installer Helm charts and hook jobs. Motivation: tenants with large numbers of tenant-scoped init hooks were occasionally triggering concurrent heavy migrations and image pulls that caused network/registry rate limits and prolonged upgrade windows. This PR adds (1) a scheduler that assigns hook execution windows and priority buckets to init hooks via values.yaml configuration, (2) deterministic template dependency graph resolution so hooks declared with 'requires' ordering are applied consistently across helm renders and upgrades, and (3) an optional init-rate-limiter sidecar controller integration that coordinates cross-tenant concurrency using a ConfigMap-backed lease. The change includes: - new values schema fields (hookGroups, priorityBuckets, templateDeps, rateLimiter) with validation in values.schema.json - Helm template helpers for rendering hook annotations, leader-elector labels and per-tenant retries - A CRD-compatible annotation convention so the private-installer can surface hook group statuses to the installer controller (note: does not introduce a new CRD in this PR; uses annotated ConfigMaps for compatibility) - Unit and integration tests for template rendering and scheduler policy - Migration notes to preserve previous installs and a small upgrade hook to mark existing non-annotated hook resources for controller adoption Backwards compatibility: defaults keep existing behavior (all hooks single group, FIFO within group). Operators that opt in get controlled ramping and deterministic ordering. See linked Linear tasks and in-PR checklist for rollout steps.

title: Introduce contextual ballot evaluator and safety-snapshot regression gate
preview:
Motivation: CI-driven functional regressions occasionally pass simple metric thresholds but still cause low-probability failure modes (safety, hallucination) to regress. This PR adds two complementary CI primitives: a Contextual Ballot Evaluator (CBE) that runs configurable re-samples of model responses and decides pass/fail by consensus ballots weighted by semantic similarity and prompt cohort importance; and a Safety Snapshot Gate that captures classifier score distributions at merge time and raises soft-fail alerts when tail mass shifts beyond tolerance.

What this change does: Adds a ballot aggregation engine with pluggable weighting (semantic similarity, precedent weight, and token-length normalization), a CI hook that executes N reruns in parallel with staggered seeds and computes vote distributions, and a safety snapshot artifact that is stored alongside CI artifacts for historical comparison. The gate emits structured signals consumed by the eval orchestrator to trigger soft quarantine or canary-focused rollouts.

Implementation notes: The ballot evaluator is implemented in eval_harness/core/ballot_evaluator.py with deterministic pseudo-random seeding and a replayable audit log. The CI hook lives in eval_harness/hooks/ci_ballot_hook.py and integrates with existing smoke runner. Safety snapshots are compact JSON braindumps capturing classifier score quantiles and cohort-level anchors and are emitted by eval_harness/hooks/safety_snapshot.py. Unit and integration tests added under tests/ to validate aggregation logic and snapshot diffing.

Checklist:
- [x] Ballot evaluator core logic and weighting strategies implemented
- [x] CI hook and pipeline integration added (ci/pipeline.yaml changes)
- [x] Safety snapshot emitter and historical comparator implemented
- [x] Tests added: unit, integration, snapshot regression simulation
- [x] Docs: docs/eval-playbooks/ballot_and_safety.md with operational guidance

Related work: builds on prior CI gating work (ENG-7203) and the contextual anchor patterns in ENG-7321. Adds an alternative to strict-threshold gates by providing probabilistic consensus and explicit snapshot-based drift detection.

Commit summary: Implement ballot aggregator and CI hook; Add safety snapshot writer and comparator; Add tests and mock fixtures; Update CI pipeline to run ballot hook on slow smoke; Add playbook docs with example linear job and runbook.

Merge decision: After two rounds of review and a CI rerun for flaky test coverage, reviewers approved and the PR was squashed and merged.

title: staggered-concurrency-simulator-and-kernel-affinity-replayer
preview:
Motivation: Our existing workload runners produce steady-state and bursty traffic, but they don't model situations where multiple client cohorts arrive in staggered waves with different concurrency profiles and pinned kernel/NUMA affinities. This PR introduces a new simulator and reporting pipeline that: 1) generates multi-cohort, staggered-concurrency workloads (wave-based arrival) with configurable inter-wave gaps and tail-heavy sub-cohorts; 2) models kernel affinity and scheduler-level contention across heterogeneous serving nodes (GPU+CPU mixes) so we can attribute latency spikes to kernel-level queuing vs runtime scheduling; 3) outputs compact heatmaps and per-kernel flamepoint snapshots compatible with our telemetry exporter so downstream dashboards can surface contention hotspots and affinity misconfigurations.

What this PR changes (high level): - benchmark-lab/sim/staggered_sim.go: new wave-based workload generator with deterministic seeding and cohort config parsing. - benchmark-lab/sim/kscheduler.go: lightweight kernel-affinity emulator that can simulate pinned kernels, soft-affinity and preemptive reshuffle. - benchmark-lab/report/affinity_heatmap.py: reporting script that ingests compressed traces and emits per-kernel heatmaps and aggregated contention scores used by the perf dashboard. - benchmark-lab/harness/staggered_e2e.sh: e2e runner used in CI and perf lab runs, includes fixture priming and KV-cache warmup steps. - benchmark-lab/tests/staggered_sim_test.go and scheduler unit tests to validate determinism and distributional properties. - CI: added a perf profile job in .github/workflows/bench.yml that runs the harness in a short smoke mode.

Design notes: - The simulator supports a JSON cohort definition: arrival_rate, wave_delay_ms, concurrency_profile (p50/p95 burst multiplier), and affinity_hint (none/cpu-bound/gpu-pinned). The PR intentionally keeps the format compact and includes a compatibility shim for older YAML configs. - Kernel affinity is modeled probabilistically: we simulate the likelihood of kernel co-location and resulting queuing by tracking per-core token buckets and an adaptive wait multiplier. This keeps the runtime lightweight while producing actionable signals for the profiler. - Reporting produces two artifacts: a compressed per-epoch kernel-heatmap (suitable for long-term storage) and a short-lived flamepoint snapshot (useful for immediate triage). Both are tagged with run metadata so they can be correlated with model, sequence length distribution, and batch sizes.

Testing and validation: - Unit tests validate deterministic seeds, distribution moments (mean/variance) of inter-arrival times, and scheduler backpressure behaviors. - Smoke e2e mode (in CI) runs a single 60s wave and verifies no panics, validates output artifact schema, and uploads a small sample to a staging S3 bucket (mocked in CI). - Local performance runs (recorded in the PR comments) show a reproducible p99 spike when two GPU-pinned cohorts overlap, which matches our hypothesis about kernel co-location effects.

Migration/compatibility: Backwards compatible. Existing workload configs are accepted via the shim; new affinity hints are optional. No changes to production serving code are included here — this is an offline benchmark and reporting addition.

Checklist: - [x] Simulator implementation and unit tests - [x] Affinity scheduler emulator - [x] Reporting scripts + example output - [x] CI smoke workflow - [x] Docs (short README in sim/) - [x] Perf notes attached in the PR comments

Related work: This complements the seq-awareness and burst-runner projects (ENG-2198) by explicitly modeling kernel-level contention; it also feeds telemetry suggested by ENG-2450 for affinity-aware dashboards.

Next steps: - Wire heatmap outputs into an internal dashboard job for automated daily aggregation. - Add a targeted multi-host version that can coordinate affinity hints across machines (future PR).

title: Introduce staggered probing schedule and failure-decay gate
preview:
Summary: Adds a time-staggered probing scheduler and a failure-decay gate to the eval CI pipeline. The staggered prober spreads probe execution across configurable windows to avoid thundering on sensitive model endpoints and to produce smoother rolling signals. The failure-decay gate introduces an exponentially decaying penalty for isolated failures so that transient noise does not immediately block rollouts.
Motivation: We observed several incidents where atomic spikes in flakiness (network blips, transient infra GC, tokenization flakiness) caused immediate hard failures in the regression gate, triggering noisy rollbacks. This change reduces false-positive blocks while preserving sensitivity to sustained regressions.
Design: - StaggeredProber: new scheduler that accepts a probe set and a window configuration (slot_count, jitter_seed, priority_buckets). It assigns probes to slots deterministically by shard key (prompt set id) with optional random jitter to smooth correlations. - FailureDecayGate: tracks per-probe failure scores and computes a smoothed failure rate using exponential decay (alpha-configurable). Gate fails only when the decayed failure rate exceeds threshold for N consecutive evaluation windows. - Integration: The prober plugs into the existing eval orchestrator as an alternate fanout mode; the gate is evaluated as part of the cluster of CI hooks after aggregated metrics step.
What changed (high level): - Added evaluator/scheduler/staggered_prober.py (core scheduling logic). - Added hooks/gates/failure_decay_gate.py (gate implementation and configuration). - Augmented orchestration: eval_orchestrator now supports --probe-mode staggered and plumbs a per-run window id. - New config: ci_defaults.yml has staggered defaults and decay params. - Tests: unit tests for scheduling determinism, integration test that simulates transient failures and verifies gate behaviour.
Rollout/flags: Default is disabled. Feature flag eval.staggered_prober enabled for canary fleets. FailureDecayGate enabled behind eval.failure_decay boolean; we will turn on for shadow runs for 72 hours before gating mainline merges.
Testing performed: - Local unit tests + mocked clock deterministic tests (added). - End-to-end integration in staging with a synthetic noisy backend reproduced the old false-positive pattern; the new gate retained sensitivity to sustained regressions while ignoring isolated spikes. - Load test: staggered mode reduced peak concurrent probe workers by ~68% under our synthetic sharded workload.
Observability and telemetry: - New metrics: eval.staggered.slots_utilization, eval.staggered.skew_conflict_rate, eval.failure_decay.decayed_rate, eval.failure_decay.window_alarms. - Added dashboard panels and SLO alert tweaks in observability-pack to avoid duplicate alerts during initial rollout.
Backwards compatibility: Existing probe modes (batch, immediate) unchanged. Gate is additive and can be toggled per-project.
Linear/Jira: Implements ENG-3421 (reduce noisy regression blocks) and ENG-3570 (probe scheduling improvements); related to SUP-8891 (customer-reported flakiness).
Risks and mitigations: - Misconfiguration could delay detection of true regressions: mitigated by conservative default thresholds and gradual rollout. - Scheduler determinism across workers: covered by deterministic sharding tests and cross-checked with end-to-end runs.
Checklist: - [x] Unit tests added - [x] Integration test added - [x] Docs updated (ci/README section) - [x] Feature flags wired - [x] Telemetry emitted
Release notes: "Introduce staggered probing schedule to reduce probe thundering and a failure-decay gating mechanism to reduce false-positive CI blocks. Disabled by default; enable via feature flags for canaries."

title: Introduce tenant zone-locker middleware, operator KMS lease shim, and audit-stripper profiles
preview: Context: Customers in regulated regions require per-tenant enforcement that incoming requests are pinned to an allowed geo zone and that any KMS operations use an ephemeral operator lease tied to the asserted zone. This PR implements a lightweight "zone-locker" middleware to normalize/respect tenant zone assertions, an operator KMS lease shim to create short-lived operator-scoped credentials for cross-tenant KMS orchestration, and an "audit-stripper" profile that redacts sensitive context from high-volume logs while preserving compliance anchors. Motivation: reduce blast radius for cross-region operator actions, make KMS usage auditable and proofable, and lower PII exposure in high-cardinality audit streams. Changes include: 1) request-time zone assertor that enforces tenant zone from token/headers with configurable soft/hard modes; 2) operator KMS lease flow that leverages existing CMK allocator but issues ephemeral operator leases with signed residency assertions; 3) pluggable audit-stripper pipeline stage with configurable redaction profiles per-route. Checklist: - [x] design doc linked: internal/doc/tenant-zone-locker.md - [x] unit & integration tests added - [x] staged rollout flags (feature gate) - [x] documented runtime knobs in CLI and Helm - [x] linear tasks closed: ENG-4182, ENG-4190 Notes: This is intentionally conservative in scope (does not change default enforcement behavior). See internal runbook for rollback and emergency disable of zone-locker and operator-lease feature gates. For more details see https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4182 and internal doc at docs/security/tenant-zone-locking.md

title: Enforce credential-usage-policy: KMS claim translator & compact audit zipper
preview:
Motivation: tighten operator credential usage and KMS selection so that operator/agent tokens cannot be used to access keys outside approved jurisdictions, while keeping audit trails compact and verifiable. This PR adds a lightweight RBAC v2 credential-usage policy evaluator, a KMS claim translator that maps operator claims to region-bound key selectors, and a compact audit zipper that collapses repetitive audit entries while retaining a cryptographic verification header for later replay. These features together address customer requests for stricter jurisdictional controls (Linear ENG-2148) and an incident postmortem recommendation to reduce audit storage costs without losing forensic detail (SEC-1203).

What this changes: single continuous description below —
- RBAC: add new policy predicate credential_usage.allowed_actions and integrate into decision path for operator-scoped tokens. The evaluator runs as an early deny guard in request admission (priority before quotas/ratelimit). Default deny-if-absent semantics for cross-region operations.
- KMS claim translator: a new translation layer that consumes token operator_claims.region_hints and service tags to select KMS key aliases with region affinity. It emits a signed kms_selection_proof header that downstream keystore proxies verify; translation includes a fallback chain (primary region -> tenant region -> global soft-fail) configurable by policy.
- Region pinning: request routing honors the translator's region_hint; requests with explicit residency flags are pinned to matching capacity pools and a non-matching route results in a structured audit event + rate-limited denial to avoid silent fallback.
- Compact audit zipper: stream-side component that collapses repeated audit lines (same actor/action/resource within configurable window) into a single compressed envelope while emitting an audit_index entry. The envelope includes a Merkle-root-like digest and a timestamped nonce; raw events are stored for 72 hours then replaced by the envelope for long-term retention to satisfy e-discovery while saving S3/DB costs.
- Telemetry & observability: add metrics for denied_credential_usage, kms_translation_latency, audit_zipper_compression_ratio, and a per-tenant residency_pin_rate. Traces include the translator decision span and the audit zipper span for post-incident timelines.

Checklist (implemented):
- [x] RBAC predicate + admission hook
- [x] KMS claim translator and signed selection header
- [x] Routing pin enforcement and structured deny event
- [x] Audit zipper compression logic and retention lifecycle
- [x] Unit + integration tests for policy and translator
- [x] Migration guide and feature flag (off by default)

Compatibility/rollback: Feature gated under feature.flags.credential_usage_enforcer. Rolling back flips the gate and reverts request admission to previous path; translator has a "passthrough" mode to preserve behavior during canary. Migration notes are in docs/security/credential-usage.md.

Related: linear ENG-2148, SEC-1203, and internal runbook RBAC-R1. No customer-facing API changes; header kms_selection_proof is additive and will be ignored by older proxies.

title: Tolerate trailing newline in API key and improve auth error messaging
preview: Context: SUP-4902. Customers reading API keys from mounted files often include a trailing newline. The Go SDK previously validated the API key string strictly and returned a generic 'invalid api key' error, causing confusion. Changes: if key ends with CR/LF, trim only those characters; if other whitespace exists, return a clearer error message pointing to the whitespace; add unit tests; update README guidance for Kubernetes secret mounts. Release: v0.6.2.

title: Add language‑agnostic presubmit adaptor and shrinkwrap CI runner
preview:
Context: Our presubmit surface has accumulated language-specific runners and duplicated caching logic (JS/TS, Go, Python). This increases maintenance cost and causes inconsistent preflight behavior. This PR introduces a small, language-agnostic presubmit adaptor plus a CI-side shrinkwrap locker to reduce dev dependency churn and shrink the presubmit footprint.

Goals: unify presubmit invocation across languages, centralize cache & auth plumbing, reduce CI setup time per job, and add a deterministic shrinkwrap snapshot for devdeps that CI can use to stabilize installs. Avoid feature creep: adaptor is intentionally minimal and implements a plugin discovery contract for linters/test runners rather than bundling language tools.

What this change does (high level):
- Add tools/presubmit/adaptor: a lightweight Go binary that discovers language plugins, normalizes tool exit codes, and streams logs in Redwood CI JSON format.
- Introduce .ci/shrinkwrap-locks/ and scripts/ci/shrinkwrap-locker.sh to generate and validate a shrinkwrap.json per workspace during a nightly job; CI uses this to fastpath install.
- Replace language-specific workflow steps with a single presubmit-adaptor step in .github/workflows/presubmit-slim.yml; adapter loads small per-repo plugin manifest.
- Add a minimal Docker image for ephemeral presubmit runners to reduce VM setup time.
- Documentation and migration guide at docs/ci/presubmit-adaptor-migration.md.

Migration and rollout plan:
1) Merge adapter behind a feature flag in CI and enable on a small set of repos (redwood-examples, redwood-sdk-typescript).
2) Nightly shrinkwrap job seeds locks for those repos.
3) Gradually flip presubmit workflows to call adaptor and observe presubmit latency/IO metrics in the CI dashboard.
4) Expand to remaining repos once stable.

Testing: Unit tests for plugin discovery and exit-code normalization included; integration smoke job added to Github Actions that runs the adaptor image against a sample mono-repo. Benchmarks: initial rollout shows median presubmit setup time reduced ~22% and cache hit-rate improved for node-based repos.

Backwards compatibility: adapter understands legacy runner env vars and can be switched off by reverting workflow references. No breaking API changes.

Notes: This PR intentionally does not change how model-serving repos run long integration suites. It's focused on quick presubmit feedback loops and developer experience improvements.
